top_items.title: Show HN: AI Simulaionen Based on FEP

definition: A unifying theory proposing that any self-organizing system that resists disorder must act and perceive so as to minimize a quantity called variational free energy, an upper bound on the surprise of its sensory inputs, so perception, learning, and action all become instances of the same optimization. What makes it distinctive is active inference: there are two ways to reduce free energy, change your beliefs to match the world (perception) or change the world to match your beliefs (action).

body.text: The free energy principle is an ambitious attempt to state a single imperative that every persisting biological system obeys. Its claim is that to keep existing, an organism must keep its sensory states within the narrow range compatible with survival, which is equivalent to minimizing the long-run surprise of what it senses. Surprise itself is hard to evaluate directly, so the brain is said to minimize a tractable proxy borrowed from statistical physics and machine learning: variational free energy, a quantity that mathematically upper-bounds surprise. Drive free energy down and you implicitly drive surprise down.

The principle was formulated by Karl Friston in the mid 2000s and grew out of his earlier work on predictive-coding. Its formal heart is variational inference, the same approximation that powers the variational-autoencoder. A system carries an internal model that encodes beliefs about the hidden causes of its sensations, and variational free energy measures the gap between those beliefs and reality as a trade-off between how well the model fits the data and how far its beliefs have strayed from its priors, a balance expressed through kl-divergence. Minimizing this bound is provably the same operation as approximate bayesian inference, which is why the framework is sometimes called a process theory for the bayesian-brain.

What makes the principle distinctive is that it folds action into the same objective, under the name active inference. There are two ways to reduce free energy: change your beliefs to match the world, which is perception and learning, or change the world to match your beliefs, which is action. An agent moves its eyes, its limbs, or its body precisely to gather the sensations its model predicts, so behavior becomes self-fulfilling prophecy in the service of minimizing surprise. This dissolves the usual boundary between inference and control, and it reframes goals and rewards as prior expectations about preferred states rather than as a separate value signal.

For artificial intelligence the free energy principle offers both a foundation and a research program. It recasts perception as the kind of inference that generative models already perform, and it suggests an alternative to standard reinforcement learning in which exploration and exploitation emerge from one drive: agents seek out informative, uncertainty-reducing observations because resolving uncertainty lowers expected free energy. Active-inference agents have been built for control and navigation tasks, and the framework continues to influence thinking about curiosity, world models, and sample-efficient learning. Critics note that the principle is so general it can be hard to falsify, but as a unifying lens connecting thermodynamics, Bayesian inference, and adaptive behavior, it remains one of the most discussed ideas at the border of neuroscience and AI.

image.alt: Schematic of the perception-action loop as overlapping ovals: Environment States (external) eta on the left, Model States (internal) s on the right, and in the middle a shared boundary of Sensorial states o and Active states a, with curved arrows showing information flowing from environment to senses to model and from model to actions back to the environment.
image.caption: Under the free energy principle, an agent is separated from its environment by a boundary of sensory and active states. Hidden external causes eta produce sensations o, which update the internal model states s (perception), and the agent emits actions a that change the environment to fit its predictions (active inference). Both perception and action serve one goal: minimizing variational free energy, an upper bound on sensory surprise.
image.credit: Pietro Mazzaglia, Tim Verbelen, Ozan Catal, Bart Dhoedt
